interactions.summary: Jiang frames the lecture around three Crusades questions, then begins with the Christian reversal of older religious wealth logic: early Christianity makes humility and poverty the route to heaven.

He poses the central contradiction: the church built on poverty becomes the wealthiest religious organization in history, partly because Europe's post-1000 agricultural and urban revival produces wealth and inequality.

Jiang models the church as a control form stronger than empire: empire commands labor through a mortal ruler, while the church commands soul, heart, mind, and eternal fate through God, Latin, hierarchy, and a faith monopoly.

claims.claim: Early Christianity is presented as a religion of humility and poverty that negates wealth by promising heaven rather than worldly favor.
The Catholic Church's historical power is paradoxical because a religion founded on poverty became immeasurably wealthy.
After the year 1000, climate, agricultural tools, trade, and urban growth make Europe wealthier, but that wealth also produces inequality and the high point of feudalism.
claims.temporal_scope: Medieval background as presented in the 2025-03-20 lecture.

claims.claim: Jiang argues that the church pioneered a new control technology: unlike empire, it governs through an eternal, infallible God and can threaten eternal damnation.
Latin, priestly authorization, and hierarchical bureaucracy are treated as mechanisms that tell ordinary people theology is not for them.

signature_moments.moment: Christianity reverses older sacrifice logic: wealth no longer proves divine favor; giving it up becomes the path to heaven.
signature_moments.why_it_matters: This reversal makes the later wealth of the church the lecture's founding contradiction.

signature_moments.moment: The church has 'more money than God' inside a religion supposedly built against wealth.
signature_moments.why_it_matters: The phrase gives the episode a sharp image for institutional contradiction.

signature_moments.moment: Empire can kill the body; the church can threaten the soul forever.
signature_moments.why_it_matters: It defines the difference between political coercion and religious world-control.

glossary_terms.definition_or_usage: The church's claim to be the only true religion, giving it control over salvation, orthodoxy, and social obedience.
Jiang's description of the medieval church as a top-down administrative hierarchy comparable to empire.

chronology_notes.note: The post-1000 agricultural and urban revival is used as background for church wealth and feudal inequality.

uncertainty_notes.note: A short ASR-damaged phrase appears after Muslim Spain; it does not affect the surrounding control-model argument.
